title: Ἔλαφος ἐπὶ νάματος καὶ λέων
titleEn: The Stag at the Spring and the Lion
source: Émile Chambry, Ésope: Fables, texte établi et traduit (Les Belles Lettres, Paris, 1927), fable 102 (= Perry 74). Text as printed, including Chambry's editorial punctuation (« », ⟨ ⟩ for editorial supplements). Pronunciation: reconstructed Attic, after W. S. Allen, Vox Graeca. Romanization: standard classical transliteration (ē/ō; th/ph/ch; y for υ, u in diphthongs; h = rough breathing; ēi/āi/ōi for iota subscript; accents omitted).

sentences.t: Ἔλαφος δίψῃ συσχεθεῖσα παρεγένετο ἐπί τινα πηγήν·
sentences.tr: Elaphos dipsēi syschetheisa paregeneto epi tina pēgēn:
sentences.l: A deer, by-thirst gripped, came to a certain spring:
sentences.i: A deer, tormented by thirst, came to a spring.
sentences.n: συσχεθεῖσα — the same 'gripped (by thirst)' that brought the goat to the fox's well (δίψει συνεχόμενος). The fables share their openings the way folktales share formulas.

sentences.t: πιοῦσα δέ, ὡς ἐθεάσατο τὴν ἑαυτῆς σκιὰν ἐπὶ τοῦ ὕδατος, ἐπὶ μὲν τοῖς κέρασιν ἠγάλλετο, ὁρῶσα τὸ μέγεθος καὶ τὴν ποικιλίαν, ἐπὶ δὲ τοῖς ποσὶ πάνυ ἤχθετο ὡς λεπτοῖς οὖσι καὶ ἀσθενέσιν.
sentences.tr: piousa de, hōs etheasato tēn heautēs skian epi tou hydatos, epi men tois kerasin ēgalleto, horōsa to megethos kai tēn poikilian, epi de tois posi pany ēchtheto hōs leptois ousi kai asthenesin.
sentences.l: and having-drunk, when she-beheld the of-herself reflection on the water, over the horns she-exulted, seeing the size and the intricacy, but over the feet greatly she-was-vexed, as thin being and weak.
sentences.i: As she drank, she caught sight of her reflection in the water — and gloried in her antlers, admiring their size and their branching, but was thoroughly ashamed of her legs, so thin and feeble they seemed.
sentences.n: σκιά as 'reflection' again, exactly as in the Dog Carrying Meat. The balanced ἐπὶ μέν … ἐπὶ δέ weighs antlers against legs — pride and vexation in mirrored clauses; ὡς + participle gives her own opinion of the legs ('on the ground that they were…').

sentences.t: Ἔτι δὲ αὐτῆς διανοουμένης, λέων ἐπιφανεὶς ἐδίωκεν αὐτήν· κἀκείνη εἰς φυγὴν τραπεῖσα κατὰ πολὺ αὐτοῦ προεῖχεν· ἀλκὴ γὰρ ἐλάφων μὲν ἐν τοῖς ποσί, λεόντων δὲ ἐν καρδίᾳ.
sentences.tr: Eti de autēs dianooumenēs, leōn epiphaneis ediōken autēn: kakeinē eis phygēn trapeisa kata poly autou proeichen: alkē gar elaphōn men en tois posi, leontōn de en kardiāi.
sentences.l: And still she pondering, a lion having-appeared pursued her: and-she, to flight having-turned, by much him outstripped: for the-strength of-deer in the feet, of-lions in heart.
sentences.i: While she was still musing, a lion appeared and gave chase; she turned to flight and left him far behind — for a deer's strength is in its legs, a lion's in its heart.
sentences.n: The narrator's aside ἀλκὴ γὰρ … is verbless Greek at its tersest: 'strength of deer in feet, of lions in heart.' καρδίᾳ here means fighting spirit, not anatomy.

sentences.t: Μέχρι μὲν οὖν ψιλὸν ἦν τὸ πεδίον, ἡ μὲν προθέουσα διεσῴζετο· ἐπειδὴ δὲ ἐγένετο κατά τινα ὑλώδη τόπον, τηνικαῦτα συνέβη, τῶν κεράτων αὐτῆς ἐμπλακέντων τοῖς κλάδοις, μὴ δυναμένην τρέχειν συλληφθῆναι.
sentences.tr: Mechri men oun psilon ēn to pedion, hē men protheousa diesōizeto: epeidē de egeneto kata tina hylōdē topon, tēnikauta synebē, tōn keratōn autēs emplakentōn tois kladois, mē dynamenēn trechein syllēphthēnai.
sentences.l: So-long-as bare was the plain, she, running-ahead, kept-being-saved: but when she-came to a certain wooded place, thereupon it-happened, the horns of-her having-been-entangled in-the branches, that, not being-able to-run, she-was-caught.
sentences.i: As long as the plain was open she ran ahead and kept her lead; but when she reached a wooded stretch, her antlers tangled in the branches, and unable to run, she was caught.
sentences.n: συνέβη + infinitive once more — with a genitive absolute (τῶν κεράτων … ἐμπλακέντων) tucked inside it. The geography is the argument: open plain for the legs, woodland for the antlers.

sentences.t: Μέλλουσα δὲ ἀναιρεῖσθαι ἔφη πρὸς ἑαυτήν· « Δειλαία ἔγωγε, ἥτις ὑφ' ὧν μὲν προδοθήσεσθαι ἔμελλον, ὑπὸ τούτων ἐσῳζόμην, οἷς δὲ καὶ σφόδρα ἐπεποίθειν, ὑπὸ τούτων ἀπόλλυμαι. »
sentences.tr: Mellousa de anaireisthai ephē pros heautēn: « Deilaia egōge, hētis hyph' hōn men prodothēsesthai emellon, hypo toutōn esōizomēn, hois de kai sphodra epepoithein, hypo toutōn apollymai. »
sentences.l: And being-about to-be-killed she-said to herself: « Wretched I, who by-what-things I-expected to-be-betrayed, by these I-was-saved, and in-what-things I even greatly had-trusted, by these I-perish. »
sentences.i: At the point of death she said to herself: « Wretch that I am — what I thought would betray me was my salvation, and what I trusted most is my destruction. »
sentences.n: Her last words are a double chiasmus of relative clauses: ὑφ' ὧν … ὑπὸ τούτων / οἷς … ὑπὸ τούτων — betrayers-turned-saviors, trusted-turned-killers. ἐπεποίθειν is pluperfect of πείθομαι with present force: 'I had (long) trusted.'

sentences.t: Οὕτω πολλάκις ἐν κινδύνοις οἱ μὲν ὕποπτοι τῶν φίλων σωτῆρες ἐγένοντο, οἱ δὲ σφόδρα ἐμπιστευθέντες προδόται.
sentences.tr: Houtō pollakis en kindynois hoi men hypoptoi tōn philōn sōtēres egenonto, hoi de sphodra empisteuthentes prodotai.
sentences.l: So often in dangers the suspected of friends saviors have-proved, and the greatly trusted, traitors.
sentences.i: So it often proves in danger: the friends we doubted save us, and the ones we trusted most betray us.
